headings.text: Ocean heat content anomaly, top 2000 m · 1960–2024
Global marine heatwave days per year · 1980–2024
Every decade since 1880, ranked by colour.

paragraphs.text: Since 1880 the global ocean surface has warmed an average of +1.05°C. In 2023 that long, patient climb broke into a sprint — and the heat is settling deeper than ever recorded.
Set the warmest sea-surface temperature ever measured — and then 2024 broke it again, by another 0.18°C across the tropical band.
ARGO float profile range

1880–2024
        ERSSTv5 · HadSST4
        IAP OHC v3
        ARGO 2005–

Global sea-surface temperature, expressed as the deviation from the 1901–2000 mean. Below baseline reads as cool blue; warm anomalies climb through amber into deep red beyond +1.5°C. The signal is monotonic after 1976.

Annual SST anomaly values relative to the 1901–2000 climatology. Each pre-1940 decade sat below the baseline; every year since 1985 has sat above it. The post-1976 inflection marks the modern thermal regime.
Source · NOAA NCEI ERSSTv5, HadSST4 (Hadley Centre)

The 2023 line departs from the historical envelope in April and never returns. By August, sea-surface temperature exceeded the 30-year maximum by margins climate scientists had not modelled as possible before 2050.
Source · NOAA OISST v2.1, Climate Reanalyzer (UMaine)

The August 2023 anomaly sat 4.6 standard deviations above the 1982–2011 daily mean — a deviation with a sub-1-in-a-million expectation under stationary climate assumptions. The climate is not stationary.

Surface temperature is only the top millimetre of the story. 91% of trapped greenhouse heat is absorbed into the ocean, accumulating downward through the upper 2000 metres. Each basin tells a different version of the same accumulation.

The Pacific has absorbed the largest absolute share of accumulated heat — roughly 41% of the global signal — but the Southern Ocean is warming fastest relative to its volume, with implications for Antarctic bottom water formation.
Source · IAP/CAS Cheng et al. 2024, NOAA NCEI
Equivalent to detonating 5 Hiroshima-scale energies per second, continuously, for 53 years.
Atmosphere absorbs 1%; land 5%; cryosphere 3%. The ocean carries nearly the entire energy imbalance.

Warming concentrates near the surface, sharpening the boundary between the warm mixed layer and the cold deep ocean. A steeper thermocline suppresses vertical mixing, starves the surface of nutrients, and traps heat in shallower water.
Thermocline (20°C isotherm) sat at ~140 m. Mixed layer averaged 60 m. Surface SST 26.4°C.
Thermocline rose to ~108 m. Mixed layer thinned to 38 m. Surface SST 27.6°C. Stratification index +18%.
Annual count of marine heatwave days, aggregated globally. Decadal mean has risen from 28 days/yr (1980s) to 152 days/yr (2020s). 2023 set a new record at 248 days; 2024 surpassed it.
Source · Hobday et al. 2018 framework · NOAA Coral Reef Watch · IPCC AR6 WG1 Ch.9

SST anomalies derived from the ERSSTv5 reconstruction (NOAA NCEI) gridded at 2°×2°, with HadSST4 cross-validation. OHC integrated 0–2000 m using IAP/CAS gridded product with bias-corrected ARGO float profiles from 2005 onward, XBT-corrected prior. Thermocline analysis uses the World Ocean Atlas 2023 climatology and ARGO trajectories binned by basin.

Pre-1950 SST records carry ±0.08°C uncertainty from sparse shipboard sampling. OHC uncertainty halves with ARGO deployment (2005). Decadal trends reported to 95% confidence. All anomalies relative to the 1901–2000 climatological mean unless otherwise noted.
An infographic journal translating peer-reviewed ocean science into visual narrative. Independent, non-commercial, open data.

Since 1880 the global ocean surface has warmed an average of +1.05°C . In 2023 that long, patient climb broke into a sprint — and the heat is settling deeper than ever recorded. Tropical Pacific · 1990 Equatorial cross-section · °C MIXED LAYER · 60 m 15°C ISOTHERM · 140 m 0 m 100 300 500 1000 120°E 90°W Thermocline (20°C isotherm) sat at ~140 m. Mixed layer averaged 60 m. Surface SST 26.4°C. Tropical Pacific · 2024 Equatorial cross-section · °C MIXED LAYER · 38 m 15°C ISOTHERM · 108 m 0 m 100 300 500 1000 120°E 90°W −32 m Thermocline rose to ~108 m. Mixed layer thinned to 38 m. Surface SST 27.6°C. Stratification index +18%. Metric 1990 2024 Δ Trend Mixed layer depth, m 60.4 38.1 −22.3 Shoaling 20°C isotherm, m 142 108 −34 Shoaling Surface salinity, PSU 34.62 34.18 −0.44 Freshening Stratification N², ×10⁻⁴ s⁻² 3.41 4.02 +0.61 Strengthening Nutricline depth, m 95 72 −23 Shoaling The ocean is not warming uniformly — it is sharpening. A thinner mixed layer means more concentrated surface heat, more frequent marine heatwaves, and a thicker barrier between life-supporting nutrients below and photosynthesis above. — IAP Annual Report, 2024 Fig. 05 / Marine Heatwaves Days Of Extreme Heat +54% since 1980 Global marine heatwave days per year · 1980–2024 ≥90th percentile, 5-day persistence (Hobday et al. method) 120 80 40 0 2023–24 · 240+ DAYS 1980 1995 2005 2015 2024 Annual count of marine heatwave days, aggregated globally. Decadal mean has risen from 28 days/yr (1980s) to 152 days/yr (2020s). 2023 set a new record at 248 days; 2024 surpassed it. OHC integrated 0–2000 m using IAP/CAS gridded product with bias-corrected ARGO float profiles from 2005 onward, XBT-corrected prior. Thermocline analysis uses the World Ocean Atlas 2023 climatology and ARGO trajectories binned by basin. Uncertainty Pre-1950 SST records carry ±0.08°C uncertainty from sparse shipboard sampling.
